data.title: Gladstone Commercial's Pivot To Industrial Properties Still Has Its Dividend Yield At 9%
data.description:
Gladstone Commercial's (NASDAQ:GOOD) shift of its property portfolio mix away from offices towards industrial properties was meant to reduce its inherent risk profile, theoretically driving dividend yield compression. That has not happened. The equity REIT last declared a monthly cash dividend of $0.10 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $1.29 per share annualized for a 9% dividend yield. This is around 45 basis points higher than its 4-year average dividend yield and 2x its sector median of 4.46%. REITs are broadly priced for risk, with higher-risk securities generally trading at higher yields than those with a lower-risk profile. At the core of GOOD's heightened risk profile is the safety of the dividend. The REIT reduced its dividend by 20.3% at the start of 2023, and currently has an elevated leverage profile with a debt-to-assets ratio that has risen to its highest point since the pandemic. I last covered GOOD in April.

GOOD generated second-quarter revenue of $39.53 million, up 6.7% over its year-ago comp and a small beat of $1.21 million on consensus estimates. The REIT owned 143 properties spread across 17 million square feet in 27 U.S. states and with a focus on secondary markets with higher cap rates. Its leases as of the end of the second quarter had an average remaining term of 7.1 years. During the second quarter, GOOD acquired two fully-occupied properties spread across 519,093 square feet of rental space for $79.3 million. This was at a weighted average cap rate of 8.88%, with the purchase partially offset by the sale of a single non-core office property for $5.1 million. Critically, there was a 67% allocation to industrial properties and a 29% allocation to offices as of the end of the second quarter, a dramatic change from the summer of 2021, when office properties accounted for roughly 48% of GOOD's portfolio.

FFO Growth And Dividend Coverage

GOOD generated second-quarter funds from operations ("FFO") of $0.33 per share, down by 3 cents versus its year-ago comp of $0.36 per share. The dip was driven by higher interest expenses on total debt of $798.89 million held as of the end of the second quarter. This drove an interest expense of $10.1 million, growth of 6.3% over its year-ago comp. I like that GOOD's occupancy ended the quarter at 98.7%, up 30 basis points sequentially from 98.4% in the first quarter. This instills confidence in the portfolio, further bolstered by 51% of its tenant base having an investment-grade or investment-grade equivalent credit rating.

GOOD's coverage of its dividend at 110%, a roughly 91% payout ratio, is decent and could be set for incremental improvements on the back of pending Fed rate cuts. The CME FedWatch Tool currently has the probability of a 25 basis points cut at the September 17 Federal Open Market Committee meeting at 96%, the highest percentage this year for a meeting just a couple of weeks out. GOOD is well placed to benefit from this is the short-to-medium term, with $474.37 million, just under 60%, of its total debt being held at a variable rate range of term SOFR + 1.30% to 1.35%. GOOD also has two outstanding preferred shares, the Series E (NASDAQ:GOODN) and the Series G (NASDAQ:GOODO), both trading at a yield on cost of around 7.3%.

Debt Maturities And Liquidity

Bears would be right to mention that GOOD has hedged the bulk of its floating rate debt, limiting the near-term impact of cuts. The REIT's overall debt maturity profile is also relatively front-loaded, with 55.7% of its total debt coming due for repayment from 2026 to 2027. This will be a mix of mortgage, line of credit, and term loan maturities. The rise in net debt-to-gross assets to 46.5% is high but not too alarming against the broader backdrop of high interest rates. I'd expect to see GOOD's FFO rise in response to rate cuts. The REIT managed to push through a same-store rents increase of 6.4% in the six months prior to the end of the second quarter.

Conclusion

GOOD's move towards industrial properties has not been reflected in a lower dividend yield on what should have been a perceived reduction in the risk profile of its diversified property portfolio. The current dividend is well covered, and pending rate cuts should provide a near-term boost to FFO despite the bulk of variable debt being hedged. The REIT sold 50,540 shares of its common shares under an existing at-the-market program to raise $700,000 during the second quarter, with its cash and cash equivalents ending the quarter at $12 million. The controversy here is that management is offering new equity with the common shares trading close to 52-week lows. This is problematic because the 9% dividend yield is more than the cap rate on recent property purchases. Hence, GOOD is being rated a hold.

data.title: C3.ai: Looking Like A Victim Of AI With Negative Growth And Non-GAAP Operating Losses
data.description:
C3.ai (NYSE:AI) was falling on Thursday after releasing first quarter results that were more or less in-line with the preliminary release from last month. The company has seen a sudden reversal in its growth trajectory, with management blaming bad execution. The company maintains its typically large cash hoard, but remains a ways away even from non-GAAP profitability. The valuation, even after the fall from recent highs, remains elevated given the poor growth and profitable profile. I reiterate my sell rating for the stock.

AI Stock Price

I last covered AI in November 2023, where I reiterated my sell rating mainly based on valuation. The stock has underperformed the broader market by around 86% since then.

While this period of significant underperformance has indeed reduced the valuation multiple, the growth profile has also deteriorated since then and I see no reason to lift my bearishness.

C3.ai Stock Key Metrics

C3.ai is an enterprise software company which helps customers implement artificial intelligence use cases. When it came public during the pandemic, a crucial part of the investment thesis was the idea that it had “partners” which would sell the product for it. This remains true even today.

The latest quarter saw revenues decline 19% year-over-year to $70.3 million, with the non-GAAP operating loss coming in at $57.8 million. These results were largely communicated in the preliminary release in August.

I note that the company saw its GAAP operating loss widen significantly, and the company is one of the few software names in my coverage universe that has not made meaningful progress on profitability since the 2022 crash in tech stocks.

C3.ai ended the quarter with $712 million of cash vs. no debt, representing a strong balance sheet which will be tested over the coming quarters and years given the non-GAAP losses.

Looking ahead, management guided for the second quarter to see up to $80 million in revenue, representing between 15% to 24% YoY declines. Management also projects the non-GAAP operating margin loss to come in at around 63%.

On the conference call, management called these results “dreadful.” Chairman Thomas Siebel noted that the poor execution is “probably 70% sales disruption and 30% my not being as involved in the details as I've previously been.” There's likely some truth in that, but at this point it bears remembering that this remains a very small company with what's looking to be an unproven product as compared to competitors.

Is AI Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, C3.ai traded at around 5.5x sales. Consensus estimates call for a material acceleration in growth moving forward.

Those consensus estimates look optimistic as there's no obvious sign that growth may return so aggressively. Furthermore, this is not the first time that growth has reversed for this company and it's not positive that this is occurring during a period in which many AI software names have finally seen growth accelerate meaningfully. The strong net cash balance sheet might make the stock look cheap to some, but I caution against overlooking the importance of the steep non-GAAP operating losses. I would expect ongoing losses to lead to either a continued reduction in the cash balance or increase in shares outstanding, or both. With growth now turning negative, I expect the company to see minimal progress on improving profit margins, if any. Even ignoring the non-GAAP operating losses and poor growth rates, the stock does not look cheap relative to software competitors like Okta (OKTA) or Adobe (ADBE) which are showing robust top-line growth with GAAP profitability. I have a suspicion that the valuation, even after the crash from recent highs, is still being supported in part by the trendy stock ticker.

AI Stock Conclusion

I wish I could be more optimistic here given the significant underperformance. However with both the top and bottom lines moving in the wrong direction, all with the valuation still looking aggressive, it may be time for investors to acknowledge that C3.ai might not be thriving in the new age of artificial intelligence. Competitors are showing meaningful acceleration in growth rates, suggesting that the issues might be more than just execution-related. I reiterate my sell rating for the stock. I suggest investors to instead look for bargains among high quality names, as I have done for the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio.

data.title: Salesforce: A Unique Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Salesforce, Inc. (NYSE:CRM) easily beat analysts’ consensus expectations for earnings and revenue for the second fiscal quarter on Wednesday. The CRM company continued to see a lot of momentum for its agentic AI services, Agentforce, which now accumulated more than 6,000 paid deals since the service was kicked off in the second half of last year. The software firm's shares, however, dropped off 6% in the after-hours market as Salesforce’s Q3 ’26 outlook disappointed investors.

The company is still seeing double-digit top line momentum, however, and has considerable potential to grow long term, especially as AI product adoption rises. CRM shares are very competitively valued on the drop, and I recommend investors to start a position following the earnings-related correction.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Salesforce a strong buy in my last work in May -- A Steal For Growth Investors -- as I liked the CRM company’s potential in the Cloud and AI market. Further, Salesforce generated a massive amount of free cash flow, which underlines its position as one of the most free cash flow profitable SaaS companies in the market. In the second-quarter, Salesforce generated strong growth for its agentic AI product and I believe investors are overreacting to the Q3 '26 outlook.

Salesforce crushed Q2 ’25 earnings

The CRM applications provider beat bottom and top line expectations by good margins in the second fiscal quarter on Wednesday: the AI software company reported $2.91 in adjusted earnings per-share, beating the consensus estimate by a solid $0.13 per-share. The top line also beat expectations, coming in at $10.2B, surpassing the consensus estimate by $100M.

Salesforce is seeing solid Agentforce adoption, revenue growth still slow

Salesforce had a solid second quarter, which was defined by momentum for its AI products, especially with regards to its agentic AI service Agentforce which the company rolled out at scale in September 2024. In this service alone, the software company added 12,500 new clients since its launch, about 6,000 of which resulted in paid deals for the Salesforce now.

Salesforce generated 10% year-over-year top line growth in the second-quarter, led by segments such as Platform and Integration & Analytics which are in especially high demand by the company's enterprise customers. Platform generated 16% Y/Y revenue growth while Integration and Analytics posted 12% Y/Y top line expansion. I am especially hopeful about the Integration/Analytics business because I see the strongest long-term growth runway here, given increasing adoption of artificial intelligence.

Salesforce is making a heavy bet on AI and the company has a leading position in agentic AI in the customer relationship management market. The general agentic AI market is set for rapid expansion as enterprise consumers adopt artificial intelligence to drive business insights and productivity. According to Precedence Research, the agentic AI market is set for explosive expansion in the years ahead, with the market intelligence firm estimating that the market will expand to $199.0B by FY 2034, implying an annual average growth rate of 44% annually.

Besides growth upside in agentic AI, I see Salesforce's free cash flow profitability as a very good reason why investors would want to buy yesterday's drop.

In the second fiscal quarter, the CRM applications provider generated $605M in free cash flow on total revenues $10.2B, leading to a solid free cash flow margin of 5.9%.

The second and third fiscal quarters tend to be weaker quarters from a FCF standpoint for Salesforce as companies typically renew (and pay) for their software plans in Q1 and Q4 of each fiscal year. While Salesforce’s FCF margin dropped off in the second fiscal quarter compared to last year, the CRM platform is still a widely profitable software platform: in the last trailing-twelve months period, Salesforce generated $12.5B in free cash flow on total revenue of $39.5B, showing a free cash flow margin of 31.6%.

Given how much free cash flow the company's software platform generates, I believe we could see more AI-driven acquisitions in the future which could potentially allow Salesforce to accelerate its top line growth.

Weak guidance for Q3 '26 created negative sentiment overhang

While the CRM platform beat bottom and top line estimates and sees a decent amount of revenue momentum, in part related to the roll-out of Agentforce, the company submitted a weak forecast for the third-quarter: here, the software company expects 8-9% top line growth on a year-over-year basis and revenue between $10.24-10.29B... which disappointed Wall Street. Analysts expected $10.29B in revenue which equaled the firm’s high-end guidance point. The weak guidance for Q3 ’26 revenue explains why shares of the CRM platform dropped 6% in after-hours trading.

'Buy the drop' opportunity

Given Salesforce’s strong position in the customer relationship management software market as well as the platform's solid free cash flow strength, I believe investors should consider buying the drop. Shares of Salesforce are currently valued as a forward (FY 2026) price-to earnings ratio of 20.3X which compares to a historical, 3-year average P/E ratio of 22.3X.

Microsoft (MSFT) and Oracle (ORCL), which are also running major software businesses, are trading at forward price-to-earnings ratios 27.7X and 27.5X... and the industry group average currently sits at 25.2X. Salesforce is still expected to generate the strongest long term EPS growth in the industry group at 17.4% which makes the company still a very intriguing growth play, especially with a growth catalyst like agentic AI in the firm's portfolio. For comparison, Oracle, which also offers enterprise CRM services, is expected to generate 15.7% annual EPS growth growth going forward and its shares are 35% more expensive than Salesforce's.

Salesforce could revalue to a 25.0X earnings multiplier, in my opinion, assuming that the company maintains adoption momentum for Agentforce and delivers on stronger-than-average EPS growth in the years ahead. Salesforce is also widely profitable based off of free cash flow and achieved FCF margins north of 30% in the last trailing-twelve months period... which limits risks for investors and downside for the shares, in my opinion. A 25.0X price-to-earnings ratio, applied to a consensus FY 2027 EPS estimate of $12.63, implies a fair value somewhere around $316/share.

Risks with Salesforce

The biggest risk for Salesforce is a potential slowdown in customer acquisition for its Agentforce service offering which is key to the company’s strong growth right now. A spending slowdown in the enterprise market in terms of agentic AI would likely be a severe headwind for the software platform. What would therefore change my mind, and potentially my stock rating, about the CRM platform is if Salesforce were to see weakening AI product adoption on the enterprise side or a deteriorating free cash flow profile.

Final thoughts

Salesforce had a strong second fiscal quarter, defined by solid momentum in the AgentForce offering... which saw a record amount of paid contracts in Q2 '26. Despite lower free cash flows in Q2, due to seasonal reasons, the CRM platform remained widely profitable as well... which I believe is Salesforce's ultimate strength: the company generates so much free cash flow that it has the resources to pursue value-adding acquisitions and boost its top line growth through M&A activities. While the outlook for Q3 '26 disappointed, shares of Salesforce are undervalued and the agentic AI opportunity represents a massive long term growth lever for the company.

data.title: The Fed Is A Soap Opera...
data.description:
Manole Capital Management

The Federal Reserve

September 2025

The Fed as a Soap Opera?

Who would have thought the Federal Reserve could rival a soap opera for drama? Chairman Powell, first appointed in 2018, has already faced a pandemic, the sharpest recession in decades, and the highest inflation since the 1970s. Now, political attacks from President Trump have made the Fed’s independence a front-page story.

Congress gave the Fed a dual mandate, to ensure price stability and full employment. However, this was granted with substantial discretion to transact. Without congressional support, the Fed’s ability to credibly execute its dual mandate could be at risk. Independence has never been absolute - Presidents have always preferred easier money—but today’s pressure campaign is unusually public. History shows the danger when the Fed bends to politics. In the 1970s, President Nixon pressured Fed Chairman Burns for easy money to juice the economy before reelection. Burns later wrote that President Nixon’s pressure was “startling and even frightening.” When Burns acquiesced to Nixon’s demands, the result was double-digit inflation.

The Fed’s independence has never truly been absolute, as it exists and serves “at the pleasure of Congress.” It’s convenient to say the Fed is independent, but it operates within the political system. Politicians and officials are always looking to get elected, and it is obviously much easier in a booming economy than in a recession. As former Fed Chairman Alan Greenspan once said, “I cannot remember many calls from presidents or Capitol Hill for the Fed to raise interest rates.”

How the Fed Sets Interest Rates:

The Fed Funds rate - the short end of the yield curve - is set by the Fed. This is the central bank’s most consequential decision, spurring or hindering bank lending and determining whether the economy expands or contracts. The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) - 12 members who meet 8x a year—sets this rate. The rest of the yield curve is determined by market forces. Regional Fed banks act like quasi-private institutions but fall under 12 governors appointed by the President and confirmed by the Senate.

The chair sets the agenda for FOMC meetings and leads the institution but cannot force policy decisions on the committee. Turmoil could ensue if the Chair loses majority support. While the Fed has traditionally spoken with one voice, cracks are appearing. We wonder what would happen if the FOMC had large disagreements with its existing chair. Is this the start of a schism? In an organization that prides itself on consensus, where disagreements are rare, the last FOMC vote had two dissenters—its first since 1993. While this was just two dissenting votes, could other governors begin to voice a differing opinion?

In early August, Fed governor Adriana Kugler resigned ahead of schedule, giving President Trump an immediate appointment. Kugler did not give a reason for her early departure, other than saying she plans on teaching at Georgetown University. Then, on August 26th, President Trump demanded Fed Governor Lisa Cook resign over alleged mortgage fraud or face removal. While the Federal Reserve Act allows removal ‘for cause,’ the law doesn’t define exactly what that means, leaving it to judicial interpretation. Lawsuits—and possibly the Supreme Court—will likely settle this dispute. Is this just one step in President Trump’s desire to restructure the entire Fed? This might be the beginning of a broader executive branch interference program to control the Fed’s operations.

We expect a more unpredictable Fed, which means more volatility (which would certainly help volumes on our exchanges). We never want to see financial markets get riled up, but we expect additional uncertainty, potentially some institutional instability, and heightened volatility. Markets so far remain calm, but sustained political pressure could erode confidence in the Fed’s independence. For now, it feels like Wall Street’s version of popcorn theater, but the implications are serious.

What Change at the Fed Could Look Like:

Since his 2024 re-election, President Trump has been demanding lower rates. He repeatedly mocks Chairman Powell as “Too Late,” a “numbskull,” and even a “stubborn MORON.” Steering monetary policy while receiving verbal attacks from the White House probably wasn’t what Powell envisioned when he joined the Fed. Powell’s term ends on May 15 , 2026, and given tensions, we question if he’ll last that long.

One of the first shifts at the Fed could be messaging and communication. The current Fed avoids surprises, holding press conferences, publishing forecasts, and regularly signaling its thinking. Economists once complained that the Fed made decisions “behind the curtain,” but now critics argue it telegraphs too much, making it easier for markets to game its moves. As the World Bank President said in June, “Today’s Fed is effectively the world’s biggest hedge fund.” Under President Trump, expect more abrupt economic announcements, perhaps even via Truth Social, X.com, Instagram, and Facebook.

The Potential Candidates for Fed Chairman:

Now, let’s highlight some of the potential candidates President Trump will choose from for Powell’s successor. Yes, you can finally legally bet on important things, like whether the Miami Dolphins will win Super Bowl 60 in Santa Clara, California, on February 8th, 2026 (currently at 10,000:1 on DraftKings), or who will be the next Fed Chairman.

According to prediction market Kalshi, the best candidates are:

  • 31% Christopher Waller, Fed Governor, nominated by President Trump in 2020
  • 21% Kevin Warsh, a former Fed Governor from 2006 to 2011, and now an active investor
  • 18% Kevin Hassett, National Economic Council Director and macroeconomist specializing in taxes
  • 10% David Zervos, Jefferies Chief Marketing Strategist
  • 4% Scott Bessent, current Treasury Secretary, has asked to be removed from consideration
  • 2% Michele Bowman, Fed Vice Chair of Supervision

We correctly predicted Powell back in October 2017 (see here), and our view is that Kevin Warsh seems like President Trump’s most likely choice. He was considered in 2018, has the credentials, and is echoing many of President Trump’s critiques. On CNBC in July, Warsh argued, “We need regime change at the Fed. It’s not just about a person. It’s about an approach to economics. We need to change how we’re thinking about inflation, how we’re conducting policy, how we’re supervising and regulating banks, and how we’re communicating.” Warsh has been blunt; he said the current Fed “isn’t working,” and he advocates for “breaking some heads” at the central bank. At a conference in April, he quipped, “There are only two problems with data dependence, the data and the dependence.” Clearly, this is not the language of past, cautious central bankers.

One thing is certain: whoever takes over next year will push for rate cuts and a major overhaul. The administration insists the Fed misunderstand inflation’s drivers and has mishandled policy. Expect a radically different framework for inflation and interest rates.

The Fed has evolved from a rate-setting body into an institution that shapes fiscal policy and influences the global economy. Today’s Fed is driven by a profound sense of caution. Tighten too quickly, and you could hinder potential growth; tighten too late, and inflation accelerates. Could a new-look Fed alter its fundamental mission altogether? Might it begin to resemble the political divisions of the Supreme Court? Regardless of who leads, change is coming—either in how the Fed defines inflation, conducts policy, or communicates with markets. The Fed was a creation of the political system, and it can be altered and changed by it. That change is coming in the next few months and quarters.

The Current Macro Picture:

After Chairman Powell’s 14-minute Jackson Hole speech, markets rallied (the S&P 500 rose +1.5% and the Russell 2000 increased by +3.7%) on hopes for a September 25-bps cut. But when Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent floated a 50-bps cut, speculation spiked further. Is the Fed acting in the economy’s best interest or bowing to political pressure?

Investors must remember that the Fed often follows the data rather than leads it. Cuts typically reflect existing conditions, not create them. Several risks remain for the “data dependent” Fed. First, tariffs could hit consumers and producers harder than expected, and cutting too aggressively risks fueling inflation—especially with the S&P 500 at record highs.

Inflation has cooled from its 2022 peak of +9.1%, but with Core CPI above 3% and producer prices rising (highest monthly increase in 3 years), pressures remain. Meanwhile, unemployment has edged up to 4.2%. While not alarming, the trend is creeping higher and higher. When the BLS (Bureau of Labor Statistics) reported this uptick, President Trump questioned its accuracy and promptly replaced its Commissioner – Erika McEntarfer. So much for “don’t fire the messenger.”

The current Fed sees itself as institutionally nimble and intellectually flexible. Critics see it as bloated, backward looking, and outdated. Powell must steer the Fed through 9 months political hostility while convincing markets the Fed remains data-driven, not politically driven. As former Chairman Bernanke said, “The Fed’s credibility - its perceived willingness to make hard decisions based on data and nonpartisan analysis - is an important national asset.” That credibility is now being tested more than at any point in the last 50 years.

Conclusion:

According to the CME's FedWatch tool (a favorite resource of ours), markets see an 86% chance of a 25-bp rate cut in September and nearly a 50% chance of another in December. While the bond market may worry about the implications, equity markets, IPOs, and M&A activity typically welcome a lower-rate environment.

Still, the upcoming cut is no surprise. Markets have already priced it in, which means the Fed’s decision is unlikely to spark a major move. History shows rate cuts rarely change the market cycle’s direction. In 2001 and 2007, they couldn’t stop bear markets, with stocks falling (40%) before bottoming. In 1974 and 1990, by contrast, they happened to coincide with the start of new bull markets. On average, after Fed easing cycles, equities rose +7% over six months and +12% over twelve months. While stocks have often risen after initial cuts, the true driver has always been the economic and earnings cycle. Lower rates help, but fundamentals, not Fed decisions, drive sustainable rallies.

That is why we focus on fundamentals, not Fed drama. A small and well-telegraphed cut may steepen the yield curve slightly, but it won’t alter the long-term trend. In the current environment, we largely see a resilient US economy, even as some signs of slowing are starting to show. Market performance ultimately depends on earnings growth, consumer resilience, and global demand, not a quarter-point move.

As investors, our job is to look past the noise, whether it’s central bank theatrics or political headlines. We stay disciplined, patient, and focused on cash flows and fundamentals. That approach has served us well, and we believe it will continue to deliver value in the years ahead.

Warren Fisher, CFA

Founder & CEO

Manole Capital Management

warren@manolecapital.com

DISCLAIMER:

Firm: Manole Capital Management LLC is a registered investment adviser. The firm is defined to include all accounts managed by Manole Capital Management LLC. data.title: Relatively Loose Macro Conditions Remain A Tailwind For Stocks
data.description:
By James Picerno

Identifying why the stock market trends one way or the other is a slippery affair, especially in the short term, but a broad measure of macro “liquidity”1 conditions is usually on the short list of relevant influences. Perhaps, then, it’s no surprise that the recent macro bias on this front (defined below) has been favorable, which suggests it’s been a key reason for the stock market’s recent resilience despite the potential for headwinds from tariffs and hints that economic growth is slowing.

All else equal, equities are relatively vulnerable when macro conditions are tight, as they were to a relatively extreme degree going into 2022 - a rough year for stocks, as shown below.

Coincidence? Unlikely. Macro liquidity alone may not determine the path for equities, but it’s often a crucial variable.

There are, of course, multiple factors at any given time that move the market, each with varying degrees of influence, depending on the state of affairs at each point in time. Although it’s impossible to prove a counterfactual, it’s reasonable to assume that if macro conditions had been tighter recently, the market would have struggled to rebound from the April tariff shock.

When macro liquidity skews negative, as it eventually will at some point, stocks will face a tougher road for keeping the bulls running. Other factors may offset tighter conditions, and so it’s never clear how or when a shift in macro conditions will trigger a change in market sentiment.

This much is clear: macro liquidity deserves to be monitored as a potential early sign that the bias is transitioning from bullish to bearish, or vice versa. On that basis, it appears that peak liquidity has passed for the current cycle. Although conditions have turned relatively tighter, a clear liquidity bias remains intact.

That doesn’t mean the stock market can’t suffer an extended correction in the current climate, but at the moment, it’s likely that factors other than macro liquidity would be the catalysts that derail the bullish trend.

The Macro Liquidity Indicator in the chart above uses five inputs to assess conditions: the US dollar, the high-yield bond spread, commercial bank loans and leases, consumer inflation and the US 10-year Treasury yield. There are many variations on measuring this risk factor, but the mix shown above is a sensible first approximation.

___________

1 The term “liquidity” is used here in reference to overall monetary and financial conditions, and not to be confused with actual market liquidity.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: Salesforce: The Market Hates The Guidance, I Like The Value
data.description:
Introduction

Salesforce, Inc. (NYSE: NYSE:CRM) is a tech-focused company that has become a giant in cloud-based software, as pictured by the massive Salesforce tower in San Francisco. The company is known for its complete suite of CRM (Customer Relationship Management) products that can offer strong tools for businesses of all sizes and categories. These particularly focus on sales, marketing and customer service.

With Salesforce just releasing its quarterly earnings, I want to explore if the company is still a viable growth company that can be purchased at the right price, especially considering its recent profitability push plus its slew of AI solutions.

Q2 earnings and Current Dynamics

Salesforce just announced a beat on both its top and bottom line with a 9.8% Y/Y growth in terms of sales, reaching $10.24B, a beat of $100MM. Regarding its Q2 EPS, the company beat the consensus at $2.91, up 13 cents from what was expected. That was driven by impressive demand for its suite of products, plus management highlighted their “agentic enterprise” strategy that focused on binding AI and human capabilities. This has translated into a massive growth in the company’s Data Cloud and AI segments at around 120% Y/Y.

The negatives came from the company’s Q3 outlook as CRM expects sales to reach between $10.24B to $10.29B an overall 9% increase Y/Y, while having Non-GAAP EPS coming in at between $2.84 to $2.86. This is overall good but the market perceived it as conservative and failing to meet high expectations. The Street remains skeptical about the immediate impact of AI on Salesforce’s bottom line with in addition to the competitive AI landscape that has seen immense capital resources poured in, with yet a significant ROIC to be shown in the case of large-cap techs. Salesforce also improved its FY adj. EPS to a midpoint of $11.35, while also expecting total revenue to reach a midpoint of $41.2B.

The high AI expectations regarding big tech companies such as Salesforce are, in this case, going against the company as it is delivering, but not as much as AI-focused companies like Nvidia (NVDA). The company is performing well in the sector with its Agentic Enterprise movement, but it highly depends on other companies adopting AI in their day-to-day operations, which have diverse businesses. Furthermore, as Salesforce grows larger, it will likely become more challenging to sustain strong growth, for which the company has become known. Given the more and more difficult dynamic of organic growth for the company, it has a poor track record in deals made with material premiums paid while facing many integration risks.

Going by offerings, it is clear that Service Cloud, Sales Cloud and the overall Salesforce Platform have been vastly entrenched in their customers’ operations, which would result in high switch costs, protecting the company’s high cash flow segments. Furthermore, these cloud solutions are highly entangled with one another, which makes the complete set more appealing for customers and boosts the company’s sales. This is why Salesforce has seen its customer retention gradually increase over the company’s lifetime and is now standing at a robust 92%, with the bulk of turnover coming from small businesses.

Regarding the CRM and Service product lines, these are simply critical to any operations due to all the information on each company’s customer base plus the integration with various other companies' departments such as accounting and marketing. If an enterprise decides to switch its CRM, this would result in a gigantic and tedious task that is simply not worth changing its system.

Valuation

If we assess Salesforce as a strong tech company with a role to play in AI integration, I believe that the company is relatively cheap with its P/E of 24.01 and its EV/EBITDA of 21.51. The company has also been able to generate FCF in recent years, with a current FCF yield of 5.1%, the highest it has had over the past decade. Furthermore, the company is currently having the lowest P/E over the same timespan as well, and it has been able to create higher earnings on the back of AI solutions and capitalizing on its entrenched position for enterprise CRM.

Growth should still continue at its robust rate thanks to the focus on international markets as well as the natural cross-selling that Salesforce can capitalize on. With the rise of AI, Salesforce has seen a tremendous rise in Net Income that was previously surprisingly volatile compared to other tech companies. I believe that this was certainly thanks to the company’s new profitability focus, and I believe that this can certainly continue as Salesforce continues expanding its AI offering, fetching higher prices.

The focus for Salesforce has clearly been on expanding the company’s margins, which has been a critical point for the company. CRM has now improved its EBITDA margin tremendously since 2022, when it stood at 15.36% to now 28.7% and the same could be observed with FCF margins reaching a massive 31.64% from the 2022 levels of 20.14%. I believe that Salesforce will be able to continue on that trend. Though Salesforce currently has poor relative return metrics as it is largely due to the legacy of the company’s aggressive deal-making, such as Slack and Tableau, which added large amounts of intangible assets and goodwill. Though with the focus on operational efficiency and incremental profits, I suspect that those numbers will be higher in the future.

Regarding capital return to shareholders, CRM just approved a $20B increase to its original buyback program, now accounting for a massive $50B. Though there is no official timeline on the program, the company has bought around $2.2B of its own stock this current quarter, so that authorization is quite flexible and is certainly long-term focused on making buybacks at accretive prices.

Risks

Salesforce’s valuation is still highly dependent on its growth in certain particular segments, mainly in sales cloud and commerce clouds, with any deceleration would have an impact on the company’s stock. Salesforce has been turning to deals in its past to drive revenue ahead, but has largely paid premiums and seen smaller growth prospects than expected. The tech SaaS will certainly continue with deals, although probably smaller than before, but will then see more competition from specialized companies that might have better advantages than a large corporation like CRM.

The company is also highly dependent on its CEO and founder, Marc Benioff, who could certainly be hard to replace as he pioneered both the software industry and corporate culture.

Conclusion

I believe that right now, based on the company’s earnings, Salesforce could be a strong value play in the current AI momentum. It has a relatively cheap valuation as well as a strong shift into profitability, which I certainly appreciate as a value investor. I rate the company as a Buy.

data.title: The Year So Far: Nothing Is Like It Seems
data.description:
Every summer, I like to step back, grab my surfboard, and let the market run its course without me for a bit. But before heading to the beach, I always review how the year is shaping up.

2025 is a perfect reminder of how investing often feels contradictory: everything and nothing is happening at the same time.

Headlines about tariffs, global conflicts, and slowing economies dominate the news. Your neighbor may still be predicting a crash every other week. And yet here we are: the S&P 500 (SPX) is up about 6% (including dividends), and the TSX 60 has returned more than 9% as of July 1st. That’s resilience.

Nothing is Like It Seems

Despite all the noise, markets are holding up—and even thriving in places. In the U.S., industrials are having a strong run, possibly helped by tariff-related reshuffling. Communications have rebounded, with Meta (META)(META:CA) leading the way and AT&T (T) finally finding its footing. Utilities, left for dead last year, are back in favor. And Financials—banks, insurers, and investment firms—are doing well too.

Meanwhile, the sectors that usually cause headaches continue to do so. Consumer Discretionary has struggled, Health Care is a mixed bag with both winners and disasters (UnitedHealth (UNH)(UNH:CA), anyone?), and Energy is back in the bottom three performers.

The economic backdrop is just as split. In the U.S., GDP growth is steady, unemployment remains low, but housing starts have slowed. Hardly the picture of an imminent recession.

Canada tells a different story. GDP growth is cooling, unemployment is ticking up, and businesses are bracing for tariff-related uncertainty. The funny part? Despite that weaker economic data, the TSX is actually outperforming the S&P 500 this year.

That’s the paradox of 2025: weak data, strong markets.

What We Can Learn

The past six months prove why you can’t let headlines drive your investment decisions. We’ve lived through dozens of “reasons to sell” in the past two decades—2008’s banking collapse, 2020’s pandemic panic, and rate hikes in 2022. Each time, the market found a way forward.

This year is no different. The market continues to chug along, and dividend investors who stayed the course are once again being rewarded.

Highlights from My Stock Picks

Even though my focus is always on the long term, I like to check in on how specific names are performing mid-year. Let’s take a quick look at six of them—three in the U.S. and three in Canada.

U.S. Picks

At the beginning of the year, I shared three undervalued U.S. stocks from my Top Stocks picks. Below is the 6-month review below.

PepsiCo (PEP)(PEP:CA)

Pepsi is wrestling with a strong dollar, higher tariffs on concentrate and aluminum, and rising operating costs. Management lowered expectations, guiding for flat EPS this year instead of mid-single-digit growth. Still, this is a company built on recurring demand and pricing power. With brands like Doritos and Gatorade, Pepsi remains a dividend growth staple, even in a sluggish year.

Mastercard (MA)(MA:CA)

Mastercard continues to deliver double-digit growth. It’s expanding partnerships with Microsoft (MSFT)(MSFT:CA) and OpenAI and rolling out new payment programs. Worries about stablecoins nibbling at the edges haven’t changed the fact that Mastercard and Visa (V)(VISA:CA) remain the tollbooths of global commerce. For dividend investors, this is the definition of a low-yield, high-growth compounder.

Automatic Data Processing (ADP)

Payroll is boring—but boring is beautiful when it’s reliable. ADP posted high single-digit growth in both revenue and earnings this year and is on track for another healthy dividend increase. Its sticky business model, with customers staying for over a decade, makes ADP one of the most dependable dividend growers you can own.

Canadian Picks

Then, I did the same in the Canadian market. Read the mid-year review below.

Stantec (STN:CA)

Stantec is finally getting the love it deserves. Long one of the best Dividend Triangles in Canada, the engineering and design giant is benefiting from global trends in climate adaptation and infrastructure renewal. Revenues, earnings, and dividends are all hitting record highs, and the backlog remains robust.

CCL Industries (CCL.B:CA)

CCL, a global leader in labeling and packaging, stumbled out of the gate in 2025 but is making a comeback. Revenues and earnings are moving higher again, helped by acquisitions, and management announced a sizable share buyback program. Quiet, diversified, and steady—CCL remains one of my favorite Canadian compounders.

Alimentation Couche-Tard (ATD:CA)

This one hurts. Couche-Tard is a core holding of mine, but 2025 has been rough. Revenue fell 7.5%, and EPS dipped 4.2%, thanks mostly to lower fuel prices and softer demand in the U.S. Merchandise sales were a small bright spot, but uncertainty around a potential 7-Eleven acquisition is clouding the outlook. Long term, I still believe in the business, but patience will be required.

Final Thoughts: Stay the Course

Halfway through 2025, investors face a strange market: headlines are scary, economic signals are mixed, and yet the indexes are quietly delivering solid returns.

The U.S. is steady, Canada is wobbling, and both stock markets are doing just fine. My U.S. stock picks have been underwhelming, my Canadian ones are stronger, but that’s beside the point.

The real lesson? Don’t let short-term noise shake your conviction. Five years from now, nobody will remember whether GDP grew 2% or 2.2% in Q2 of 2025. What will matter is that you stayed invested, reinvested your dividends, and let compounding work its magic.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bad Jobs Data: A Domino That Could Take Down Unrealistic Home And Stock Prices
data.description:
Cracks are now appearing in two major parts of the U.S. economy, and if these cracks widen, they could spread and create more issues. I see plenty of people talking about how strong the economy is and that the bull market can keep on going. I agree that there are some positive factors going on with the economy, including massive AI capex spending, and the consumer has been fairly resilient. It's also hard to ignore the massive run-up in stock prices, which are now near record high levels. However, it's important to remember that things typically look and feel great at stock market tops and also when the economy is at or near a peak.

The problem is that when economic downturns occur, it often happens suddenly, and there is a domino effect, and it seems as though many investors are not considering this right now. Let's say that the jobs market is one domino and the housing market is another. Both of these dominoes are looking very wobbly now and potentially poised to fall. If these dominoes do fall, they are likely to hit and knock down some other economic dominoes, and this could include the stock market, consumer spending, and much more. My point is that it really only takes one domino to fall, and that could cause many more dominoes to fall suddenly, even when these dominoes all looked to be stable and standing tall. Let's take a closer look at the newly developing cracks in the housing and jobs market, because one or both of these could soon be the dominoes to fall and create a downturn for stocks and other parts of the U.S. economy:

The Cracks In The Housing Market

Home prices saw significant increases before Covid because of the low interest rates, but rates went even lower just after Covid, and prices surged even higher. I remember seeing overbids on houses that were very often $200,000 to $500,000 over the asking prices, and there were some examples of even higher overbids. The rapid and massive appreciation in home prices is unprecedented, and it seems to have created a mindset among many homeowners that their house is always going up in value. However, the extremely low rates of years past are gone, as is the stay-at-home Covid dynamic, and the days of almost every house getting an overbid also seem to be over. Things for the housing market have really seemed to reverse in 2025, because homes are seeing many more days on the market and price reductions. I haven't seen a spring selling this bad for sellers in many years, and with fall and winter coming soon, home prices could be poised to go even lower, as this is a seasonally weak time period.

It now seems like market sentiment and dynamics have reversed from one where home sellers thought home prices only go up and major overbids were normal to one where price reductions and feeling lucky to get a single offer are often the case. One of the issues that has kept supply off the market is the "golden handcuff," whereby homeowners have refused to sell because they had a mortgage rate locked in that was around 3% or even less. In some cases, homeowners did not want to sell because prices kept going higher, and they could easily rent the home if they owned a second property. But now, for the first time in years, homeowners in many areas are seeing values drop, and this could create a dynamic whereby investors and especially people who own second homes or rental properties decide that they better sell now before prices go even lower. If you own a home with a low mortgage rate and have been put into the golden handcuffs situation, you might also decide to sell now before prices potentially go lower and you can just rent for a while.

All of these examples could contribute to more inventory coming onto the market in the coming months, and this could possibly put more downward pressure on home prices. If interest rates drop, conventional wisdom suggests that this could help the housing market and firm up the softening trend in home prices. However, there is a belief that homeowners that have been locked into golden handcuffs due to their low mortgage rates will finally move on if rates come down, and this could unfreeze the housing market and increase the amount of home inventory.

Some estimates suggest that private equity firms own 500,000 homes across the United States and could control 40% of the single-family home rental market by 2030. If these private equity firms decide to cash in by selling homes (before prices potentially fall), this could lead to a surge in inventory coming onto the market.

Unrealistic Home Prices

The Covid pandemic created a bubble in housing prices, which of course was also fueled by incredibly low interest rates. This pushed home prices to levels that have never been seen before, and common sense suggests this bubble is going to pop or deflate slowly sooner or later. The main reason why home prices are likely to come down is because home prices are far out of whack when you compare historical data such as rent vs. own ratios and affordability ratios. A common and long-standing affordability guideline has suggested that a home should represent about 3 times your annual income. However, the current national median home price is now 5 times the median annual income in the U.S. In addition, in some expensive coastal cities, such as Los Angeles, home prices represent more than 12 times annual income. The housing affordability issue has caught the attention of President Trump, who is reportedly going to declare it to be a national emergency. As shown in the chart below, home prices are way out of line with household incomes now, and this appears unsustainable.

Changes in Home Prices and Household Incomes

Household incomes have failed to keep up with increasing home prices

Unrealistic Stock Prices

Whether you look at charts or the fundamentals and historical valuations, the stock market appears priced for perfection. The cyclically adjusted price-to earnings ratio or "CAPE" is currently at 37. This is extremely high since historically the CAPE ratio is about 17, on average.

Warren Buffett appears to be taking a more cautious stance with stocks at such elevated levels and he has built a massive cash hoard at Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A), (BRK.B), which is just over $344 billion, as of June 30, 2025. Warren Buffett has a well-known metric called the "Buffett Indicator". This indicator takes the total value of the U.S. stock market and divides it by the Gross Domestic Product or "GDP". The current estimated value of the U.S. stock market is around $60 trillion and the U.S. GDP is nearly $30 trillion. This indicates a 200% ratio of market value to GDP, which is historically very high. The chart below also shows that the current valuation is 57% above the long-term trend.

The 5 year chart of the S&P 500 Index (NYSEARCA:SPY) below shows how far stocks have come in the past several years. The stock market appears overbought now and potentially due for a pullback. This does not look like the right time for me to be putting new money to work.

The Cracks In The Jobs Market

Achieving a soft-landing is something the Fed would like, but historically this has not always been the case. The problem is that in the past, the Fed has underestimated labor market weakness once it got started. This suggests that labor market weakness tends to generate downside momentum that lingers. The Bureau of Labor Statistics released the latest jobs report which showed that July non-farm payroll growth was just 73,000, and well below the expectations for 110,000. In addition, the payrolls for May and June were revised down by a total of 258,000. If the labor market continues to deteriorate (and I believe it will), this could lead to a recession and this would likely put additional downward pressure on home prices. The next jobs report is scheduled to be released on September 5th, and this could be pivotal for the stock market and show if labor market weakness is becoming a significant trend. I believe that additional weakness in the jobs market could be the "domino" that knocks down other dominoes such as unrealistic home and stock prices.

In Summary

The housing market and the jobs market both tend to work with momentum that is not easily broken. However, when a momentum shift does occur as it appears to be doing right now, the corrective phase is not typically quickly changed by an interest rate cut, which many investors are now expecting in September. I believe the jobs market is likely to deteriorate in the coming months, and this will put pressure on housing prices and possibly lead to a recession that also causes the stock market to fall. The valuations for home prices and stocks as well as other "assets" like crypto have led people to call this the "everything bubble". With home prices and stock valuations being seemingly priced for perfection, there could be significant downside if valuations return towards historical averages.

data.title: Caleres Comps May Improve In Q3 2025, But I Fear Margins, The Price Is Fair
data.description:
Caleres, Inc. (CAL) reported Q2 '25 results, with more negatives than positives, but nonetheless some positives.

The company continued to shed sales, but the results showed some sequential improvement from Q1, and further signaled potential breakeven or LSD growth into Q3 '25.

In terms of margins, the situation is much worse, with operating income decreasing to between 1/3 and 1/5, depending on how we read it. This is even worse after interest.

In addition, the company is going to leverage, to acquire Stuart Weitzman. This will add weight to debt, which, although not particularly unsustainable, is still a problem.

After earnings, the name trades at about $450 million, and I believe with some optimistic assumptions (flat revenues, flat margins) the company can generate about $55 million in earnings going forward, or an earnings multiple of 8x. This is fair, albeit not attractive, and I maintain a Hold.

Q2 '25 results

Continued loss of sales: The company posted sales down 3.6%, with the brand portfolio down 3.5%, and Famous down 5% (3.4% on a comp basis).

We should remember that we are coming from sales down 1% in Q1 '24, down 1.8% in Q2 '24, down 3% in Q3 '24, down 7% in Q4 '24, and down 7% in Q1 '25.

That is, the trend deteriorated significantly in Q3 '24, and now is the first quarter showing some improvement (in a less negative trend).

Terrible deleverage: Gross profit margins decreased 210bps, more in the brand portfolio (down 240bps) than in Famous Footwear (130bps). Given that SG&A includes occupancy costs, the only potential driver for gross margins down is a loss of merchandise margin, which may come from higher costs (tariffs) or from higher markdowns.

At the SG&A level, deleverage was also inevitable, with 130bps of margin loss. In this case, it is very hard not to deleverage with falling sales. Another factor against margins was the additional costs from the preparation of the acquisition of Stuart Weitzman.

Earnings collapse: Depending on how we measure earnings, they were down 60% (adjusted for one-time factors like the SW acquisition and expense reduction, severance, etc.), and down 80% (unadjusted).

Balance sheet post-SW acquisition

After quarter end, the company finalized the acquisition of Stuart Weitzman for $110 million net of cash. The company posted debt of $387 million in 2Q25, offset by $191 million in cash. After paying $110 million net for SW, net debt will increase to $306 million.

The company announced a renegotiation of its credit facility, extending the capacity to $700 million and potentially even $950 million. There were no comments about the interest spreads, which are not mentioned on the SEC filings either, unfortunately.

In my understanding, the spread is about 1pp over SOFR, or 6.5% currently.

Start of better comps ahead

The company said that comps were up 1% in August and LSD in the brand portfolio. In the Q1 '25 release, the company had said that Q2 '25 sales were down 4.5%, and the result ended up being down 3.5%.

That is, there is some space to believe in that positive result. Further, the comps clearly become easier after Q3 '25, and especially in Q4 '25.

Margins might not be that good

We will have two important impacts on margins in H2 '25.

First is the impact of tariffs, increasing the purchase price of items, and also reducing the disposable income of customers. This is a double impact on gross margins.

Second is the SG&A impact from the integration and acquisition of SW. The company will add back these expenses to adjusted earnings, but they are expenses nonetheless.

Valuation is fair on optimistic assumptions

From where we stand today, the company's TTM sales are $2.65 billion.

If we assume flat revenues going forward (an optimistic assumption considering the macro environment, an optimistic assumption considering the macro environment), plus $220 million in sales from SW (from the acquisition release), we can expect NTM sales of $2.87 billion.

For margins, we can, again optimistically, maintain a 5.5% EBITDA margin from TTM into NTM. That gives us $155 million in EBITDA, from which we have to remove $60 million in D&A and $20 million in debt (assuming 6.5% interest on $230 million in net debt). After taxes of 25%, we get to $56 million in net income.

This, I believe, is a relatively optimistic outlook, mainly considering that the company comes from more than two years of sales decreases, and that the outlook is not positive going forward.

We should also consider that, barring a collapse in sales and margins, the company will have no trouble covering interest even after the SW acquisition.

Compared to these, the company trades at a market cap of $450 million ($13.5 by 33 million shares). That means, based on my assumptions, the company trades at an 8x multiple of expected earnings.

I believe this is fair, albeit not attractive. The name has been shedding sales for years, and the outlook going forward from a macro perspective is not fantastic. Still, the price is not particularly expensive either.

I maintain a Hold, but have become more interested in Caleres.

data.title: CGDV: All You Need To Know About This Actively-Managed ETF
data.description:
Introduction To The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF

The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (NYSEARCA:CGDV), which is managed by Capital Research & Management Company (an affiliate of the California based Capital Group Companies, Inc.), began its listing history on the NYSE Arca, on February 22, 2022. CGDV, which can be accessed at an expense ratio of 0.33%, has amassed total assets under management [AUM] of over $21B over the last 3.5 years. This ETF also pays dividends four times per year (in March, June, September, and December), with the current yield working out to 1.39%.

What Does CGDV Do?

Before we get to the raison d'être of CGDV, we also felt it would be relevant to provide some broader context around the changing texture of the US ETF environment.

Basically, CGDV is yet another product from the burgeoning ‘active ETF’ club (ETFs that employ stock selection and asset allocation specialists that make active decisions on the portfolio), that is fast making its presence felt within the wider ETF landscape. Note that active ETFs, which previously only accounted for roughly one-fifth of US-listed ETFs, have now overtaken their passively-managed brethren (ETFs which simply aim to track certain indices) and now make up 51% of the +$11 trillion-sized market.

The catalyst behind their rise was the adoption of the Rule 6c-11 (more commonly known as the ETF rule) by the SEC (Securities and Exchange Commission) in 2019, which resulted in a more streamlined, standardized, and expedited regulatory route for fund issuers to get their prospective ETF products over the line.

So, coming back to CGDV per se, the primary goal is to focus on a bunch of large-cap, well-established dividend paying common stocks (at least 80% of CGDV’s portfolio consists of stocks of this ilk) that could help the ETF produce income that is at least 30% higher than the dividend yield of the S&P 500.

CGDV also places emphasis on capital appreciation, by pursuing “attractively-valued” companies that have the potential of being “good long-term investment opportunities”. Needless to say, what constitutes “attractively-valued” and “good long-term investment opportunities” could vary from individual to individual.

In CGDV’s case, it primarily relies on the investing sensibilities of certain members from an investment management firm, called the Capital Research and Management Company [CRMC] to generate value for its investors. Interestingly, CRMC adopts a multi-portfolio manager system, whereby decisions related to different parts of the portfolio are managed by different managers. With regards to CGDV, five individuals (two women and three men) are involved- Jim Lovelace, Martin Jacobs, Chris Buchbinder, Keiko McKibben, and Brittain Ezzes (more on them later).

What Are The Main Features Of CGDV's Portfolio?

Firstly, note that even though CGDV views the S&P500 as its benchmark, it covers a much narrower portfolio of just 57 stocks. However, like the S&P 500, it also tilts towards giant and large-caps which jointly account for 78% of the portfolio. CGDV also makes it a point to target stocks with market-capitalization of over $5B, so small-cap exposure is largely non-existent.

Investors may also be surprised to note that even though this is a US-centric product at its core, it also does dabble with stocks that are present in Europe, Canada, and Latin America, which currently account for 7% of the portfolio.

From a sector angle, CGDV gives you access to 11 different sectors, but the sector which stands out with over one-fourth of the total portfolio is the tech sector.

Then even though CGDV tilts towards the tech sector, which is construed to be a growth sector, large-cap value stocks still dominate this portfolio, accounting for 36% of the total holdings.

What Type Of Investor Is CGDV Suited For?

As noted earlier, CGDV’s main remit is to facilitate a yield that is better than the S&P 500, so large-cap oriented investors, who appreciate quarterly payouts that have been growing at a fairly sturdy pace (2-year CAGR of 32%), may be drawn to this product.

Having said that, we also feel that CGDV deserves a lot more attention from other segments of the investment community who are also seeking alpha, as, so far, its has maintained a remarkable track record of outperforming the S&P 500 not just on a total return basis, but even on a risk-adjusted return basis.

Since its inception over three years back, CGDV has generated returns of 76%, and is currently outperforming SPY (the ETF proxy for the S&P 500) by close to 20 percentage points!

Total returns will only give you a myopic perspective of things, as it won’t take into consideration the degree of risk involved in facilitating those returns. To get a more well-rounded view on the quality of returns one may also take into account the Sharpe ratio and the Sortino ratio of these products. The former tells you how effective a product has been in outperforming risk-free assets for the degree of total variability it has experienced. The latter also measures outperformance over the risk-free rate, but rather than total variability, it looks at the downside deviation that these products have experienced. Note that with both the Sharpe and the Sortino, CGDV comfortably outperforms SPY.

Also take note of CGDV’s comforting capture ratio particularly on the downside (81). What this tells us is that when the S&P 500 was down, CGDV only experienced 81% of the index’s losses, highlighting what a useful product it could be when risk sentiment in the market is quite weak.

Investors who pursue CGDV should also be comfortable with the fact that this is a portfolio managed by multiple portfolio managers. Given the performance so far, we see limited reason to be anxious about this facet, particularly as this is a team, which in aggregate, brings investment management experience of 168 years in total. Three out of the five are also CFA (Chartered Financial Analyst) charter holders, and four of the five have also been part of the Capital Group before the inception of CGDV (Brittain Ezzes being the exception) so there likely isn't too much friction within the team. Besides, all these individuals appear to specialize in different sectors, which helps bring a useful degree of reach and variability during the stock selection process.

CGDV, we also believe, will appeal to investors who are cost-conscious. One of the chief advantages that passively managed ETFs have over actively managed ETFs is that they are mostly priced at lower expense ratios; an ETF like CGDV helps ameliorate these concerns. In a world where your median ETF has an expense ratio of 0.5%, and actively managed ETFs are priced at 0.71% (on average), CGDV can be picked up with an expense ratio of 0.33%, which we view as very reasonable.

Prima facie, one would also be inclined to believe that a product with CGDV’s profile, would largely only appeal to long-term investors, but take note of the daily volumes that this product manages on average (in dollar terms, it is 100x that of most ETFs). The sturdy volumes seen every day also ensures that the difference between CGDV’s average bid and ask is very tight (just 0.02% in percentage terms, which is just around one-sixth the spreads of most ETFs) which means when you take a round trip (buy and sell), you can enter and exit very adroitly.

What Are The Risks Associated With CGDV?

Over the years, the US stock markets have been party to different themes of mean-reversion that play out over decades. One prospective mean-reversion theme that could take hold, and may not reflect well on CGDV going forward (since around 80% of its holdings are giant and large-caps) is the positioning of the large-cap to small-cap ratio (as represented by the Russell 1000 and Russell 2000 indices). Note that the current ratio is close to 24-year highs, and 39% above its long-term mean, leaving it susceptible to some normalization going forward.

What may also help drive a shift is the wide gap in valuations. Small-caps (as represented by the Russell 2000) can currently be picked up at a price-to-earnings discount of 34% (relative to the Russell 1000), and an even steeper discount of 52% on a price-to-cash flow basis.

Besides the large-cap tilt, CGDV also suffers from some top-heaviness. Despite covering 57 stocks, the top 10 stocks alone account for 43% of the total portfolio, putting the onus on just a handful of stocks.

We noted earlier CGDV’s strong tilt towards the technology sector; well, regardless of the growth potential of that sector, its worth noting that valuations of that sector, make it the priciest out of all sectors, with a P/E multiple approaching 30x. These multiples are also a lot higher than the sector’s historical averages.

What Are Some Of The Other Actively Managed Equity ETFs?

As noted earlier, the actively managed ETF space is fast picking up steam so there is quite an abundance of options besides CGDV. Within that broad universe, two close peers of CGDV we feel are the Putnam Focused Large Cap Value ETF (PVAL) and T. Rowe Price Capital Appreciation Equity ETF (TCAF).

PVAL is the oldest out of the lot, but has ironically accumulated the lowest AUM. This could well be because of its relatively steeper expense ratio compared to the other two. It also witnesses the greatest degree of churn every year (annual portfolio churn of over 50%). However this level of churn also plays a part in PVAL having the least degree of top-heaviness, despite covering the smallest group of stocks. Value-conscious investors will also greatly appreciate the relatively low P/E multiple of 17.3x.

TCAF has the widest reach (91 stocks, which is almost twice as much as PVAL), but it is heavily concentrated in technology, while it also faces strong top-heaviness. TCAF also tilts more towards giant-caps whereas the other two tilt towards large-caps. TCAF also differentiates itself with an annual dividend payout which doesn’t amount to much, and it also covers the priciest group of stocks. Having said that, it is also the most stable portfolio out of the lot, witnessing churn of only 14% annually.

Summary

CGDV, an actively managed ETF that follows a multi-portfolio management system, offers a yield that is comparatively better than the S&P 500, while offering a stunning track record of risk-adjusted returns. CGDV’s strong tilt towards the overbought large-caps and pricey technology sector should be monitored.

This article answers these three main questions about CGDV:

  • How is CGDV built?
  • What type of investor will appreciate CGDV and what are the risks they could face?
  • Historically, has CGDV been able to outperform the market?
